experiments.title: How using a faux forward on a high urgency campaign impacts donor conversion rates
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a faux forward for giving tuesday donors will achieve a change in donor conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/29/2022 - 11/29/2022
Giving Tuesday is generally a big day of giving for many non-profits. In order to cut through the noise, non-profits generally use a variety of tactics for their afternoon and evening resends, the most common of which is a faux forward. But does it really work?
For the Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate, we put that question to the test. For the PM version of their Giving Tuesday emails, across multiple email and donor segments, we tested whether a resend of the AM version (same copy, same subject line) would have as much impact as a faux forward, where the sender is from a different person and it looks like that person is forwarding the original, AM email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
For the Oblates, the question of whether using a faux forward tactic, during a time of year when everyone is receiving hundreds of emails, works has been answered. There was a huge increase in donor conversion rates from the faux forward, with a 73% increase and a 99.9% level of confidence.
Additionally, this experiment wielded the following interesting points:
While open rates are not a great metric to use for comparison, one of the main points of a faux forward is to get the reader to open the email. The treatment version averaged a 31% open rate, compared to 27% for the resend. When looking at performance by donor type (donor, recurring donor, non-donor) the lift continues. The donor segments saw a .61% response rate for the faux forward version versus a .5% response rate on the resend. Non-donor response rates also saw similar performance, with a 0.1% response rate on the control versus 0.2% response rate on the faux forward version.

				gift).
				This experiment produced the following results:
0% increase in traffic
				× 73.1% increase in conversion rate
				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: How asking for $10 impacts non-donor conversion rates in email.
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for a lower gift for non donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/28/2022 - 11/29/2022
Giving Tuesday is generally a big day of giving for many non-profits. With the challenge in acquiring new donors in this economic climate being felt across all nonprofits, we wanted to see whether asking a non-donor audience for a smaller, specific gift would impact conversion rates. We ran this test across 3 separate emails, starting with a pre-Giving Tuesday deployment on 11/28, and then an AM and PM send on Giving Tuesday itself.
The only change in the email copy was that the treatment version said “With a gift of $10 or more” as opposed to “With your gift…”.
Our hypothesis was that non-donors would find more value in donating $10, than a gift of any amount, and therefore convert at a higher rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment version of this email, where we asked non-donors for a gift of $10 or more to support the Oblates during Giving Tuesday, had a 65% increase in donor conversion rates, with a 99.9% level of confidence! While the volume of total gifts was higher, the average gift for the treatment version was 10% lower ($33 versus $37).
We believe that this was the result of a couple of factors:
The current economic climate makes it more appealing for a non-donor to become a donor when you are asking for a specific, small amount. By asking for a gift of just $10 to support the great work of the Oblates, donors had a higher likelihood of making a gift.The difference in the average gift could be attributed to a difference in gift arrays on the donation forms; the control version started with an ask of $25, whereas the treatment version started with an ask of $10.
Long-term, it would be valuable to keep track of these donors to see whether they retain as well as those that gave a higher average gift.

experiments.title: How adding a campaign goal to an acquisition landing page during year-end impacted results.
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a campaign goal for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/14/2021 - 01/28/2022
The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate serve poor and needy people in the United States and more than 70 countries around the world. One of their most popular eBook offers is their Lady of Lourdes Novena.
During their calendar year-end campaign, we wanted to test whether including the year-end campaign goal amount, as well as some specific value proposition language, on the Lourdes instant donation page would help drive a higher conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
There was a large drop in conversion rates on the treatment, campaign-goal oriented donation page, with a 41% decline in conversion rates and a 99.5% level of confidence.

We believe that this was due to a couple of factors. First, the Lourdes offer was not actively pushed during the campaign; most of the digital messaging on donation forms and the homepage mentioned the year-end goal. Therefore, people that landed on the Lourdes page through a generic Facebook ad might not have been aware there was a campaign going on. Moreover, the campaign goal itself, and why people should donate to it, was not very clear.

For future experiments we will test whether making the value prop clearer and more urgent will have a more positive impact on conversion rates.

experiments.title: How the email design influences clickthrough rate
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a simplified email design increase the proportion of people clicking to the landing page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/03/2017 - 03/07/2017
The simplified email resulted in a 14.8% increase to the percentage of visitors going to the landing page. Our hypothesis is that the lack of design seemed to be more genuine and less “marketing” oriented. As a result, the subscribers were more willing to read and engaged with the email once they opened it.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate were launching a new digital prayer book to kick off the Lenten season. As part of this launch, they wanted to give their house email file an opportunity to request the resource. This would serve as both cultivation of the subscribers and a new opportunity for them to give a gift once they had requested their PDF.

Historically, the email template used in donor communications was heavily designed with an organizational wrapper. We wanted to see if sending the same email message but in a stripped-down design would have an impact on the percentage of people that clicked on the link to download the prayer book.

experiments.title: How the use of an illustrated image over a real photo in a Facebook ad can impact click-through and acquisition
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which Facebook ad would acquire more email addresses
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/05/2017 - 05/09/2017
People were not only more motivated to click on the illustrated image over the real photo in the Facebook ad, but once they got to the landing page they were more highly motivated to get the offer as well. There was a 66.6% increase in emails acquired with the illustrated image over the real photo. The increase in click-through and acquisition can be attributed to the call-to-action being reinforced on the image, the congruency of the image used in the ad and the imagery on the acquisition page and because the image was more centric to the faith of the organization.
experiments.keyLearnings: The Missionary Oblates developed a Novena prayer in honor of Mother’s Day. They promoted this content offer through paid Facebook ads in an effort to acquire new names. We traditionally use images of real people in Facebook ads, but we wanted to test the image of the ad with something more illustrated. The illustrated image was also the same image used on the cover of the Novena prayers and acquisition page.

experiments.title: How adding a guided donation process to the main donation form impacted conversion rates
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that guiding a donation process for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/01/2020 - 09/02/2020
The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate serve poor and needy people in the United States and more than 70 countries around the world. As part an experiment on their main donation page, we wanted to test whether adding a visual call out for the different parts of a donation form impacted conversion results.
In this test, we added ‘Step 1, step 2, etc’ to each section of the donation form (gift array, billing info, etc) to test whether a guided process made an impact.
experiments.keyLearnings:
For this main donation page experiment, the guided 'stepped' process had a 17.5% increase in donor conversion rates, with almost 100% valid results.
The result of this experiment was interesting, in that we tested the same treatment on an acquisition offer donation page and saw the reverse results (while not validating).
Our hypothesis is that due to the much larger volume of donations on the main donation form, guiding the donors through the donation process simplified the experience for them, leading to a higher conversion rate.

experiments.title: How adding a premium to an ebook donation page impacts donor conversion rates and average gift.
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate

rows.name: Gift of any amount for Healing Prayers book

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a physical premium for donors will achieve a change in conversion rates..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/26/2020 - 08/27/2020
The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate serve poor and needy people in the United States and more than 70 countries around the world. For this experiment we wanted to test whether adding a premium offer to an eBook instant donation page would impact donor conversion rates and average gifts.
For this treatment version, we added in a premium of offering a 256-page Healing Prayers book as a thank you for a gift of any amount. The control version of this experiment had a $25 ask in order to have a votive candle lit for a donors’ intentions at the Our Lady of the Snows shrine.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Across all metrics tracked, the treatment version of this experiment led to a decrease. There was a 18% decrease in total revenue with a 97% level of confidence, and a 18% decrease in overall donor conversion rates with a 99% level of confidence.
Our hypothesis for this result is that by asking for a gift of any amount, instead of using the premium as an upgrade strategy, the impact was to drive down potential revenue. Moreover, while the premium tied into the acquisition offer itself (Prayers for a Spiritual Life), we believe that since the form copy itself did not give more details about the premium, it negatively impacted conversion rates as well.
For future experiments, we will test different premium-related treatments and strategies.

experiments.title: How asking for a monthly gift on an acquisition offer page impacts conversion rates
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for a monthly gift for donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/17/2021 - 05/07/2021
The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate serve poor and needy people in the United States and more than 70 countries around the world. The recruitment of monthly donors makes up a large part of their digital program; new monthly donors are offered the opportunity to have a candle lit at the shrine of our Lady of the Snows every month.
For this experiment which ran on an acquisition offer instant donation page, we tested whether asking for a monthly gift, instead of one-time, would impact donor conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results of this experiment were inconclusive as it was originally constructed. With a lack of tracking for monthly gifts, we were not able to validate whether there was an increase in monthly gifts with this test.
Moreover, the control version offered up a physical premium as a thank you for one's gift; the experiment version asked for a monthly gift to have a candle lit every month at the Our Lady of the Snows Shrine.
While the overall decrease in donation conversion rates was significant -- 51% decrease with a 99% level of confidence -- this experiment was not constructed to test one-time gift conversion rates. The impact of offering a physical premium likely led to the increased number of one-time donations.

experiments.title: How asking for a gift less than a previous contribution impacts a fiscal year end campaign
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for a lower gift than HPC for donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/01/2022 - 06/30/2022
The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate serve poor and needy people in the United States and more than 70 countries around the world.  In June, they announced a rebranding, where they combined 3 of the organizations that operated under the Missionary Oblates umbrella into one- The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate – USA. This rebranding also coincided with the end of their fiscal year, so as part of their fiscal-year end high urgency campaign, we focused on what the new organization would do (increased efficiencies in fundraising).
With the economic uncertainty of high inflation, prices, and potential recession, we ran a month-long campaign where we tested asking for a lower than previous gift for the donor segment, using language in our treatment variant that said :
Specifically, I am looking for << Test Donors Needed Qty >> people to give $<< Test Ask Amount >> right now. << Test First Name >> you have generously given $<< Test HPC Amount >> to us in the past, but with all the uncertainty in the world, continued inflation and high prices, I’m not asking for the same level of support. Instead, your gift of $<< Test Ask Amount >>, when combined with the << Test Donors Needed Qty >> other supporters of the Missionary Oblates, will ensure that we can continue to share the Good News of Jesus Christ to a lost and hurting world.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The overall campaign was a success for the Missionary Oblates, raising 16% more than the previous fiscal-year end campaign. For this experiment specifically, the 'downgrade' treatment version had a 62% higher conversion rate with 100% level of confidence! It also had a 4% higher average gift.
This shows that, even in a tough economic climate, people are willing to support the causes they truly believe in. And folding in the re-branding messaging made the appeal even stronger, as we were able to provide specific value statements that tied the donor closer to the organization.

experiments.title: How changing a call-to-action on a Facebook ad during a high urgency campaign increased donor conversion rates
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing a CTA button on Facebook ads for a digital audience will achieve a higher donor conversion rate. .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/10/2022 - 04/17/2022
Easter is a key fundraising season for the Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate. Our Easter/Spring high urgency campaign this year focused on a recurring gift offer, where any monthly gift of $15 or more would have a votive candle lit for the donor’s intentions every month at the Oblate’s main shrine, the National Shrine of Our Lady of the Snows.
For our Facebook ad campaign, we wanted to test whether a ‘donate now’ call-to-action button would drive a higher donor conversion rate than the ‘learn more’ button Oblates traditionally uses. Our hypothesis was that due to the urgent nature of the campaign, a donate now CTA would drive a higher conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
By changing the call-to-action button to 'donate now' during this limited-time, high-urgency campaign, there was a 50% increase in donor conversion rates, with a 98.5% level of confidence.
We believe that this is due to the nature of the campaign-if people are reading the Facebook ad copy, which is asking for a donation, they are more likely to click on the CTA button to donate and then complete their donation than to learn more about what their donation could be. In fact, the treatment version had a slightly lower click-through rate (0.9% versus 1%) than the control, thus reinforcing that people were motivated to donate when they landed on the donation page.

categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Talking to constituents like friends and partners will increase their engagement and donations
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/18/2020 - 12/28/2020
During their calendar year end campaign, the Missionary Oblates were running a sticky bar on their website promoting giving during year end. This sticky bar was placed across their website. Due to some other successes in talking to their constituents like partners in their mission, we wanted to test similar language on the year end sticky bar. The treatment spoke to website visitors as "friends". By recognizing them as such and even recognizing their prayers and support, we were able to draw people in and increase initial engagement. We see this by the fact that the click-through rate for the treatment increased by 53% with a 99.9% level of confidence. The treatment's call-to-action was softer and more conversational as well by saying, "If it's not too much to ask...". This approach aligned with the initial communal style and continued to reinforce this "friend" and "partnership" approach.
This experiment reinforces that communal language resonates well with this audience. Donors have even told the organization in the past that they see themselves as part of the mission. This presents more opportunities for testing this similar approach in other channels and deeper in the funnel.

experiments.title: How communal language on a content offer donation page changes donor conversion
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Using communal language, that includes the reader in the community of work, will increase donation conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/24/2021 - 04/02/2021
The Oblates were running a content offer that they give each year, we wanted to test communal language on the donation page. It was a communication style that we have tested with great success on sticky bars and have seen work well in emails to their donors, so the natural next step was to test it on the donation page. We took their typical copy, made it more conversational, welcomed the reader into their community of prayer, and care for their fellow men. The new copy showed how not only do the Oblates care most deeply and pray for the reader, but also that their work is an extension of the reader’s prayers and intentions. It also focused less on getting the premium for a gift, but rather allowed the premium to be a natural extension of their prayer as well and as a token of sincere appreciation for the work that they make possible through their giving.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The communal language increased donor significantly, by 56%. We believe this is because readers feel like the integral part of the mission (that they are) and they can sense the community that they are joining really truly brings improves lives. And because of that, revenue also increased by 41% with a 97% level of confidence.
Both new and returning visitors appreciated the communal language, the returning more so with a 97% increase in giving. Which make sense because they have a closer relationship with the Oblates as the language in the copy suggests. However, it still welcomed new visitors in with a 37% increase in donations at an 88% directional level of confidence.
Next we would like to test the premium as a reminder of the good they have done with their prayers and their giving to the Oblates. As well as test this more fully in email and on content offer landing pages.

experiments.title: How budget optimization impacts donor conversion through paid Facebook ads
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase donor conversion by optimizing our Facebook campaign to a longer timeframe using a larger budget?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/07/2020 - 04/07/2020
The Missionary Oblates run a significant number of paid Facebook campaigns in an effort to acquire new names and donors to their organization. Over the past year, they have learned a lot of optimization tactics through testing that have allowed them to increase their conversion rate in this channel. Recently they learned that using a “Lifetime” budget instead of a “Daily” budget in Facebook for the campaign they could increase donor conversion. Without having data behind the ideal length of time for that “Lifetime” budget, they set these campaigns to a four-week timeline and then would reset the budgets. Wanting to know and test into if this length of time was ideal, they set up a test that used the monthly lifetime budget for the control and developed an identical campaign that used a longer lifetime budget of four months. They also took the monthly spend and multiplied that by four (four month timeline) to get their budget for their treatment campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Over the four week period the test ran, we were able to validate the results and increase donor conversion (instant) by 55%! What is interesting is that even though Facebook had a much larger budget for a longer time period, the increase in donor conversion was not due to Facebook just simply increasing the volume of spend. The treatment version only spent an additional $140 than the control. Our hypothesis is that by giving Facebook a longer period of time with a larger budget it has more flexibility in how it optimizes the campaign.
We will continue to monitor this extended lifetime budget since the results from the experiment were only four weeks into the four month spend of the campaign. This will have a significant impact on the number of new donors acquired through this campaign.

We can't assume that people have officially made the decision to make a gift until they click that donate button. And we, as optimizers, have the opportunity to impact that decision all along the donation pathway, even right up to the moment a person makes the donation.

experiments.title: How a donation platform can impact donor conversion
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: What kind of impact would the donation microsite have on donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/30/2018 - 01/03/2019
The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate transitioned their donation platform in an effort to more efficiently create and change donation pages and for a better user experience. To test the impact of the new platform against the old platform we tested them against each other. The content on the donation pages remained the same and consistent so that we could isolate the platform itself.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Through the donation microsite, we were able to increase overall donor conversion on the primary donation page by 18.4%. As we dug into this a bit more, we noticed that the most significant lift came for mobile. Almost 64% of the traffic coming to your primary donation page was on mobile. The experience prior to the microsite was not optimized for this audience. Now, with the microsite, donor conversion on mobile devices has increased 64.3%!
What kind of impact can this have?
Looking back over the past 12 months, mobile traffic to the main donation page resulted in 393 donors with $12.8K in revenue.
Assuming the mobile conversion rate holds at 16% (which is the current mobile conversion rate on the microsite), this lift represents 62 more donors and $2k in additional revenue just on mobile alone on this one page. This projection doesn't include desktop traffic and the dozens other donation pages the Missionary Oblates have.
By simply creating a better user experience through a new donation page we were able to significantly increase donor conversion and overall revenue for the organization.
Again, this experiment has just been running on your main donation page. When the donation microsite rolls out to all of the acquisition pages, we think we'll see even bigger lifts.

categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase average gift and overall revenue by visually emphasizing a specific gift amount?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/16/2019 - 06/14/2019
Significant testing had been done on the Missionary Oblates primary donation page in an effort to increase donor conversion. Because of the low conversion rate from this page, we weren’t able to validate these experiments or help improve conversion on the page. Because of this, we hypothesized testing an element on the page that might impact overall revenue and average gift. For this experiment we tested visually emphasizing a larger gift amount than what we knew was the average gift on this page. To do this, we put the higher suggested gift amount in the middle of the gift arrays, put a box around it and noted that gift amount to be the “most popular”.
experiments.keyLearnings: We were able to increase overall revenue from the primary donation page by 24%. By visually emphasizing the gift and noting it as the “most popular”, we eliminated some of the decision process friction in making a gift and increased the average gift on the page. Additionally, we saw an even stronger increase in revenue (44%) for people on mobile devices. This learning has a significant impact on revenue for the organization.

experiments.title: How removing decision friction impacts donor conversion
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase conversion on the post-prayer request page by eliminating the number of offer options?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/02/2019 - 04/19/2019
The Missionary Oblates have a place on their website that allows people to submit a prayer request. After submitting their request, they are taken to a page with a donate button, along with 6 other options for premiums. In an earlier test where they tested the multiple options versus just one option – donate – they decreased donor conversion on the page. In analyzing the experiment they hypothesized that maybe the one option that they chose to offer wasn’t the right kind of offer. To continue to test into this, they hypothesized testing the multiple offers again versus one of the more popular offers – the Lourdes water. They also added value proposition to the page communicating the action they wanted people to take after submitting their prayer request and the benefit of that action and the offer.
experiments.keyLearnings: We were able to significantly increase donor conversion on the page by 141%! By narrowing the decision process down for people to the one they were most likely to select and then communicating the benefit of that decision and offer, we were able to remove friction for this conversion process and increase motivation. The results of this experiment can have a significant revenue impact for the organization. With the volume of traffic on this page, the high conversion rate with the treatment in place and an average gift of $27, the roll-out of this experiment has the potential to bring in over $10k in additional revenue every month.

experiments.title: How using the purchase conversion in Facebook instead of a name conversion goal impacts donor conversion.
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate

rows.name: Our Lady of Lourdes - Purchase Optimization

experiments.researchQuestion: Will the purchase conversion increase instant donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/21/2019 - 02/04/2019
We have been running Facebook campaigns to acquire names and donors for the Missionary Oblates for the past two years. In this time, we have found that we are able to acquire names for the organization very effectively at a very low cost. That said, we haven’t been able to convert new names into donors as efficiently as we would hope.
All of our Facebook campaigns have been optimized based on a custom conversion we’ve set up based on a person signing up for the particular content offer. This method has worked well to grow email files through paid media, but in recent months we’ve been testing a different kind of conversion goal within Facebook to see if we can not only acquire new names for the organization, but acquire new donors more effectively. Using the purchase conversion in Facebook optimizes the campaign for purchases/donors instead of new names.
We rolled this purchase conversion out for the Missionary Oblates Lady of Lourdes acquisition campaign to see the impact on instant donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: The purchase conversion helped increased instant donor conversion by 157%! This is a significant learning and shift in the way we will set up future acquisition campaigns. The purchase conversion allowed Facebook to optimize the campaign based on people who were making a donation. When someone would make a donation, it would tell Facebook to go find more people like the donor that just gave and target them. Because the purchase conversion optimized for purchasers/donors, instead of names, we did see a slight increase in the cost to acquire a name, but the benefit of increasing donations and revenue far exceeds that metric.

experiments.title: How optimizing for a “Donate” event in Facebook affects instant donor conversion
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate

rows.name: Our Lady of Lourdes - Purchase Optimization
Our Lady of Lourdes - Donation Optimization

experiments.researchQuestion: Does optimizing for the “Donate” event in Facebook increase instant donor conversion more than optimizing for a “Purchase” event?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/11/2019 - 03/04/2019
The Missionary Oblates previously validated an experiment that increased donor conversion for their content offer by optimizing for the native Facebook event “Purchase” rather than a custom conversion, or course signup. In test #10687, optimizing for purchase targeting led to a 284% increase in donors from the Lenten Prayers offer. This confirmed our hypothesis that Facebook shows ads to more donor-motivated people when optimizing for purchases. It allows them to pull in additional purchase data from Facebook instead of relying primarily on data that we give to them (for example, a list of prospects or a lookalike audience).
In this test, we utilized the native event on Facebook labeled “Donate”, to test if the same approach would increase donations even further knowing that Facebook was likely pulling in nonprofit or donor data as well as purchase data.
All other campaign elements remained the same.
experiments.keyLearnings: Optimizing for a donation in Facebook rather than a purchase increased instant donor conversion by 39%. This confirms our hypothesis that Facebook is using different criteria to optimize for a donation, potentially including nonprofit or philanthropic data in addition to demographic or purchase information, therefore providing an even more donor-motivated audience for our ads. This concept should continue to be tested in order to continue confirming this concept.

experiments.title: How shorter copy on a donation page during year-end impacts donor conversion
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: What kind of impact would the length of copy have on donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/06/2018 - 12/17/2018
During the Missionary Oblates year-end campaign, they hypothesized that shorter value proposition on the donation page might increase donor conversion. This hypothesis was formed by thinking about the motivation of donors during this time of year. During year-end when motivation is higher, maybe donors don’t need as much convincing in the copy to convert them to make a donation. We tested this approach against the longer copy.
experiments.keyLearnings: To our surprise, donor conversion significantly decreased with the shorter copy. While the sample size was really small, we also knew that this experiment could have drastic results on overall revenue so we turned it off as soon as we saw that it had validated. Even though motivation of donors is high during year-end, people still need a strong reason of why they should give to the organization. By shortening the copy, we removed too much of the reasons why a person should give. In this case, we specifically removed a lot of the evidentials of the ask. This experiment shows us how important evidentials are in the value proposition - they reinforce the reason to give.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign on a product page can impact donor conversion and overall revenue
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Would a radical redesign increase conversion on the page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/10/2017 - 08/15/2017
First, by removing the learn more button and seeing significantly more response, we confirm that the gifts themselves were self-explanatory and did not need explanation.
Second, by having multiple asks in a situation where it is unnecessary, people are less likely to make a decision.
Third, it is possible that when people click learn more, their mindset transitions from a giving mindset into a transaction mindset. When we move someone from a giving mindset into a transaction mindset by diving into the details, they realize that they could probably get the item somewhere else at better quality for significantly cheaper, instead of just quickly selecting a gift to go with their donation, much like grabbing a drink at the checkout line in a grocery store to enhance their overall experience.
experiments.keyLearnings: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate offer religious gifts for people to get if they make a donation to their organization. The religious items are arranged on a product page that shows an image of the item, the name of it and the opportunity to either “Learn More” or “Add to Basket”. We hypothesized that we might be able to increase conversion by strengthening the value proposition for the page and removing an element of friction from the giving process by taking away the “Learn More” button. We developed a treatment that changed those two variables on the page and tested it against the control.
